summary: 11 concrete items surfaced from HN discussion.

items.name: Semble
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Semble – Code search for agents that uses 98% fewer tokens than grep
items.summary: Code search for agents that cuts grep-heavy token usage.
items.why_included: HN commenters wanted real agent benchmarks and discussed Semble as a drop-in search layer for Claude Code/Copilot flows.
items.evidence: The thread highlighted CPU indexing, zero-key setup, and the claim that it beats grep+read on token use and retrieval quality.

items.name: RK3562 Debian Linux workstation
items.source_story_title: I turned a $80 RK3562 Android tablet into a Debian Linux workstation
items.summary: An $80 Android tablet converted into a usable Debian workstation.
items.why_included: Commenters liked Debian booting with most hardware working and treated it as a practical low-cost ARM desktop.
items.evidence: One commenter said Debian with most devices functional was great; others discussed what runs in 4 GB RAM and suggested lightweight stacks.

items.name: WezTerm
items.source_story_title: I turned a $80 RK3562 Android tablet into a Debian Linux workstation
items.summary: Cross-platform terminal emulator suggested for a low-RAM tablet setup.
items.why_included: It was explicitly recommended as part of a lightweight desktop stack for the Debian tablet.
items.evidence: A commenter suggested running WezTerm + tmux on 4 GB RAM instead of a heavier desktop environment.

items.name: tmux
items.source_story_title: I turned a $80 RK3562 Android tablet into a Debian Linux workstation
items.summary: Terminal multiplexer recommended for the tablet workstation stack.
items.why_included: The thread framed tmux as a practical way to save RAM and stay productive on the tiny workstation.
items.evidence: The comment recommending WezTerm + tmux explicitly tied it to fitting development tools into 4 GB of RAM.

items.name: sway
items.source_story_title: I turned a $80 RK3562 Android tablet into a Debian Linux workstation
items.summary: Wayland compositor praised as responsive on small ARM hardware.
items.why_included: A commenter used sway successfully on similar low-power hardware and described it as perfectly responsive.
items.evidence: The same tablet discussion said sway runs well on a PinePhone Pro and feels responsive enough to replace a heavier desktop.

items.name: Mercurial
items.source_story_title: Mercurial, 20 years and counting: how are we still alive and kicking? [video]
items.summary: Version control system still remembered as pleasant and user-friendly.
items.why_included: Commenters treated Mercurial as a technically solid tool that got beaten by Git’s popularity, not by design quality.
items.evidence: One commenter said Mercurial was more user-friendly while still offering the needed features and performance; another still uses it for personal projects.

items.name: CUDA Books
items.summary: Curated CUDA reading list with book-by-book recommendations.
items.why_included: The thread turned into a practical CUDA reading guide, with commenters naming specific books to start from.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended CUDA Programming: A Developer’s Guide to Parallel Computing with GPUs and criticized some other intro books as too shallow or error-prone.

items.name: Mado
items.source_story_title: Mado: Fast Markdown linter written in Rust
items.summary: Rust Markdown linter that commenters said is faster than rumdl.
items.why_included: The discussion gave a clear speed-and-scope comparison that favors Mado as a pure linter.
items.evidence: The author said Mado is older and faster per rumdl’s benchmark graph, while also noting that Mado stays focused on linting.

items.name: Ollama
items.source_story_title: Apple Silicon costs more than OpenRouter
items.summary: Local LLM runtime used on a Mac Studio over tailnet.
items.why_included: Commenters framed it as the practical local option for running models outside a datacenter.
items.evidence: One comment said they can run Ollama on a Mac Studio over the tailnet “for free,” which anchored the local-inference discussion.

items.name: A nicer voltmeter clock
items.summary: Retro meter-style clock build with strong maker appeal.
items.why_included: The comments were full of admiration and linked to earlier meter-clock builds.
items.evidence: One commenter said they always enjoy projects like this because they are artistically neat and spark ideas; another shared previous voltmeter-clock references.

items.name: Hosting a website on an 8-bit microcontroller
items.source_story_title: Hosting a website on an 8-bit microcontroller
items.summary: Tiny web server project running on an 8-bit MCU.
items.why_included: The thread celebrated tiny-web-server novelty and shared more microcontroller web experiments.
items.evidence: Commenters pointed to earlier smallest-web-server projects and said the ACE1101 version was great fun to reproduce.
